hero.tagline: Ohio's small claims courts handle disputes up to $6,000 with a streamlined process designed for quick resolutions.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Filing fee
at_a_glance.detail: Varies by court and claim amount

at_a_glance.label: Court
at_a_glance.value: Municipal or County Court (Small Claims Division)

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Appeal to Court of Appeals within 30 days

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $6,000, you must either waive the excess amount to file in small claims court or file in a higher court. Splitting one claim into multiple cases to stay within the limit is not permitted.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Ohio allows you 8 years to file a claim on a written contract and 6 years on an oral contract. Ensure you have all relevant documentation to support your claim.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 8-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend a friend $1,000, and they failed to repay it.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the agreement, such as emails, texts, or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned $2,500 to a colleague with the understanding it would be repaid in six months, but they haven't paid you back.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Document the loan agreement and any repayment attempts.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note to repay $5,000 over a year but stopped making payments after two months.
claims.notes: 8-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and records of payments made.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided graphic design services worth $3,000, invoiced the client, but they haven't paid.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Bring the invoice, service agreement, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $4,500 on their store credit account and refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide account statements and records of payment history.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $2,000 in back rent and has vacated the property without paying.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the lease agreement and records of unpaid rent.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for goods worth $1,200 with a check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Provide the bounced check and any bank notices.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a loan for $5,000, the primary borrower defaulted, and you had to pay the lender.
claims.notes: 8-year deadline from breach. Show the co-signed agreement and proof of payments made on behalf of the borrower.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Ohio's Consumer Sales Practices Act provides remedies for consumers, including potential treble damages for certain violations.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a new refrigerator for $1,500 that stopped working after two weeks, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from breach. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $200, whichever is greater; treble damages for knowing violations.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged you $500 but did not perform the agreed-upon tasks, leaving your home dirty.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from breach. Provide the service agreement and evidence of the poor service.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $200, whichever is greater; treble damages for knowing violations.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $2,000 for repairs that did not fix the issue, and the car remains inoperable.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from breach. Bring repair invoices and any expert opinions on the faulty repair.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $200, whichever is greater; treble damages for knowing violations.

claims.name: Contractor failed to complete work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $4,500 to renovate your bathroom, but they abandoned the project halfway through.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from breach. Provide the contract, payment records, and photos of incomplete work.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $200, whichever is greater; treble damages for knowing violations.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You hired a DJ for your wedding for $1,000, but they never showed up, and you had to find a replacement last minute.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from breach. Present the service agreement and evidence of the no-show.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $200, whichever is greater; treble damages for knowing violations.

claims.name: Unauthorized subscription charges
claims.example: You canceled a gym membership, but they continued to charge your account $50 monthly for several months.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from breach. Provide cancellation notice and bank statements showing continued charges.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $200, whichever is greater; treble damages for knowing violations.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $5,500 that broke down within a week, and the dealer refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from breach. Bring the sales contract, any warranties, and repair records.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $200, whichever is greater; treble damages for knowing violations.

claims.name: Misleading advertising leading to loss
claims.example: A store advertised a laptop for $800 with specific features, but upon purchase, you discovered it lacked those features.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from breach. Provide the advertisement and proof of purchase.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $200, whichever is greater; treble damages for knowing violations.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Ohio allows you 2 years to file a claim for property damage. Ensure you document the damage thoroughly and file within the statute of limitations.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver ran a red light and hit your car, causing $3,000 in damages.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from incident. Bring police reports, repair estimates, and photos of the damage.

claims.name: Neighbor caused damage to your property
claims.example: Your neighbor's tree fell onto your fence during a storm, causing $1,500 in damages.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from incident. Provide photos of the damage and any communication with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: After moving out, you discover your tenant left $2,000 worth of damage to the apartment.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery. Present the lease agreement, photos of the damage, and repair estimates.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone else's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and destroyed $800 worth of landscaping.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from incident. Provide photos of the damage and any witness statements.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left your watch for repair, and the jeweler damaged it, resulting in $1,200 in losses.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery. Bring the service agreement and photos of the damage.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Ohio provides specific statutes governing landlord-tenant relationships, including security deposit returns and property conditions.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord failed to return your $1,000 security deposit without explanation.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from violation. Provide the lease agreement and any correspondence regarding the deposit.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages and reasonable attorney's fees.

claims.name: Failure to provide itemized deductions from deposit
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $500 from your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from violation. Bring the lease agreement and any communication regarding the deductions.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages and reasonable attorney's fees.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had no heating during winter, and the landlord refused to make repairs.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from violation. Document the conditions and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord wrongfully retained tenant's property
claims.example: After eviction, your landlord kept your furniture worth $2,000 without legal justification.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from violation. Provide evidence of ownership and communication with the landlord.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages and reasonable attorney's fees.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your apartment.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from violation. Document the eviction and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent after lease termination
claims.example: You paid the last month's rent in advance, but the lease was terminated early, and the landlord didn't refund the unused portion.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from violation. Provide the lease agreement and payment records.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages and reasonable attorney's fees.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant missed two months of rent totaling $1,800 before moving out.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from breach. Present the lease agreement and records of unpaid rent.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You paid $500 to fix a leaking roof after the landlord ignored repair requests and deducted it from your rent.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from violation. Document repair requests, receipts, and communication with the landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Ohio law mandates timely payment of wages and provides remedies for employees facing unpaid wages or final paychecks.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 for hours worked but hasn't issued payment.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from violation. Provide timesheets and any communication regarding unpaid wages.
claims.damage_boost: Wages due plus 6% interest; additional penalties for willful violations.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer refused to issue your final paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from violation. Document your resignation and any communication regarding the final paycheck.
claims.damage_boost: Wages due plus 6% interest; additional penalties for willful violations.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,000 in sales commissions, but your employer hasn't paid them.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from violation. Provide sales records and any agreements regarding commissions.
claims.damage_boost: Wages due plus 6% interest; additional penalties for willful violations.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime but were only paid your regular hourly rate.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from violation. Document hours worked and pay received.
claims.damage_boost: Wages due plus 6% interest; additional penalties for willful violations.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Ohio provides avenues for individuals to seek compensation for personal injuries and property disputes.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on an unmarked wet floor in a store, resulting in a sprained ankle and $1,200 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from incident. Provide medical records and any incident reports.

claims.name: Damage to loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your camera, and they returned it with a broken lens, costing $800 to repair.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery. Document the loan agreement and damage.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A neighbor took your bicycle without permission and refuses to return it, valued at $600.
claims.notes: 6-year deadline from incident. Provide proof of ownership and any communication with the neighbor.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages plus possible treble damages for willful violations.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Ohio allows individuals to seek compensation for vehicle-related disputes, including repair issues and wrongful towing.

claims.name: Dispute over vehicle repairs
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $2,500 for repairs that didn't fix the problem, and the car remains inoperable.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from breach. Bring repair invoices and any expert opinions on the faulty repair.
claims.damage_boost: Actual damages or $200, whichever is greater; treble damages for knowing violations.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and you had to pay $300 to retrieve it.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from incident. Provide towing receipts and photos of the parking area.

exclusions.explanation: Small claims court cannot handle eviction proceedings; these must be filed in the regular division of the municipal or county court.
Disputes involving the title to real estate are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Claims for libel or slander are not permitted in small claims court.
Small claims court does not handle cases of malicious prosecution.
Claims seeking punitive or exemplary damages are not allowed in small claims court.
Divorce, child custody, and other family law matters are outside the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Issues related to wills, estates, and guardianships are not handled in small claims court.

exclusions.category: Claims against the state or federal government
exclusions.explanation: Small claims court cannot hear cases against the State of Ohio or the United States government.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Ohio Court of Claims or Federal Court

exclusions.explanation: Class action lawsuits are beyond the scope of small claims court.
Small claims court cannot order someone to do or stop doing something; it can only award monetary damages.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not permitted in small claims court; however, statutory penalties may be available for certain claims.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Actual damages or $200, whichever is greater; treble damages for knowing violations.
Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney's fees may be recoverable in certain cases, such as violations of the Consumer Sales Practices Act or wrongful withholding of a security deposit.
damages.postjudgment_interest_notes: Post-judgment interest rates are set annually by the Ohio Tax Commissioner.

where_to_file.court_name: Small Claims Division of the Municipal or County Court

venue_rules.scenario: Defendant resides or has a place of business
filing_options: County where the defendant resides or has a place of business

venue_rules.scenario: Where the cause of action arose
filing_options: County where the transaction or incident occurred
Any county where one of the defendants resides or has a place of business

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: Availability of e-filing varies by court; check with the specific court for options.

service_fees.notes: Optional; may be required if certified mail is unsuccessful.

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and may cover service fees; check with the court for specifics.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees may be added to the judgment against the defendant.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fees and reasonable service costs are typically added to the judgment against the defendant.

entries.clock_starts: Date of last payment or charge

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy stays.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention and serves as evidence of your attempt to settle.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad check claims, a 30-day written notice demanding payment is required before filing suit.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific pre-filing demand required for wage claims, but documenting requests for payment is advisable.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of damages within 30 days of lease termination; failure to do so may result in penalties.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: No specific pre-filing requirements, but documenting complaints and attempts to resolve is beneficial.

forms.name: Small Claims Complaint
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's claim against the defendant.

forms.name: Affidavit of Indigency
forms.description: Requests a waiver of court fees due to financial hardship.

forms.name: Counterclaim Form
forms.description: Allows the defendant to file a claim against the plaintiff in the same case.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Notifies the court and opposing party of the intent to appeal a judgment.

methods.name: Certified mail by clerk
methods.description: The court clerk sends the complaint and summons to the defendant via certified mail.

methods.name: Personal service by sheriff
methods.description: A sheriff or deputy personally delivers the documents to the defendant.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A private individual authorized to serve legal documents delivers the papers to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Ohio rules and the laws of the state where the defendant is located.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, service by publication may be permitted with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document attempts and seek court guidance for alternative methods.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer but must appear at the scheduled hearing to contest the claim.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even in default, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim and the amount sought.
response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, the defendant may appeal the denial within 30 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If a counterclaim exceeds the small claims limit, the case may be transferred to the regular docket.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys but are not required to be.
hearing.format: Bench trial with informal procedures; each party presents their case directly to the judge or magistrate.
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Parties must arrange for their own interpreters; some courts may provide assistance upon request.
hearing.decision_timing: Decision may be announced at the hearing or mailed within a few weeks.
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some courts offer mediation services to help parties reach a settlement before the hearing.

appeals.type_notes: The appellate court reviews the record for legal errors; new evidence is not considered.
appeals.bond_amount_notes: The bond amount is typically equal to the judgment plus interest and costs.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment cannot be appealed directly; the defendant must first file a motion to vacate.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest rates are set annually by the Ohio Tax Commissioner.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of the debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a garnishment application with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor is employed and earns above the exemption threshold.
exemptions: 75% of disposable earnings or 30 times the federal minimum wage, whichever is greater.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a court order, serve the bank, and the bank freezes and transfers funds to the court.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account.
exemptions: $425 per account; certain benefits are exempt.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: File a certificate of judgment with the county recorder to create a lien on the debtor's property.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns real property; may delay satisfaction until property is sold.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt personal property.
methods.description: Obtain a writ from the court, the sheriff seizes property, and it is sold at auction.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable non-exempt property.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to revive the judgment before the 21-year period expires.
exempt_assets: Social Security and federal benefits — exempt
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy may discharge the judgment; certain debts are non-dischargeable.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among creditors is generally determined by the order of filing or recording liens.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses may appear without an attorney in small claims court.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses must use their legal entity name; trade names alone are insufficient.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may represent themselves or send an employee.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face challenges in enforcing contracts.

county_variations.detail: Erie County charges $75 for filing a small claims complaint with one defendant and $10 for each additional defendant.
Oakwood Municipal Court charges $70 for filing a small claims complaint with one defendant and $20 for each additional defendant.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect defendant information
pitfalls.what_happens: If you sue the wrong party or provide incorrect information, the court may dismiss your case or you may be unable to collect a judgment.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the defendant's legal name and address before filing; for businesses, check the Ohio Secretary of State's records.

pitfalls.title: Missing the statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will result in dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Determine the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type and file within that period.

pitfalls.title: Insufficient evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Without adequate evidence, you may lose your case even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements to support your claim.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant is not properly served, the case cannot proceed.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow the court's rules for service of process carefully and use reliable methods.

pitfalls.title: Failing to appear in court
pitfalls.what_happens: Missing your court date can result in dismissal of your case or a default judgment against you.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Mark your calendar and set reminders; if you cannot attend, notify the court in advance.

pitfalls.title: Overlooking counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant files a counterclaim and you are unprepared, you could lose more than you anticipated.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Be prepared to address potential counterclaims and gather evidence to defend against them.

recent_changes.title: Increase in small claims monetary limit
recent_changes.description: The jurisdictional limit for small claims court was increased from $3,000 to $6,000, allowing more cases to be heard in this streamlined forum.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in small claims court. The process is designed to be accessible for individuals representing themselves.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: Most small claims cases are resolved within 30 to 90 days from filing, depending on the court's schedule and complexity of the case.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: The maximum amount you can sue for in Ohio small claims court is $6,000, excluding interest and court costs.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in your favor, provided you present sufficient evidence to support your claim.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims judgment to the Court of Appeals within 30 days of the decision.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Ohio is valid for 21 years and can be enforced during that period.

state_specific_notes: Ohio allows treble damages for certain consumer protection violations under the Consumer Sales Practices Act.
Small claims court cannot grant equitable relief; it can only award monetary damages.
Businesses can represent themselves in small claims court without an attorney.
The statute of limitations for written contracts in Ohio is 8 years, longer than in many other states.
Ohio requires a 30-day written notice before filing a lawsuit for a bad check.

interesting_findings: Ohio's increase of the small claims limit to $6,000 in 2016 has allowed more individuals to resolve disputes without the need for formal litigation.
The availability of treble damages under the Consumer Sales Practices Act provides a strong deterrent against unfair or deceptive practices.

sources.label: Ohio Rev. Code Chapter 1925 (Small Claims Division)
Ohio Rev. Code Chapter 2305 (Limitation of actions)
Ohio Rev. Code § 1925.01 et seq. (Small claims fees)
Ohio Rev. Code Chapter 1345 (Consumer Sales Practices Act)
Ohio Rev. Code § 2307.61 (Theft and bad check civil penalty)
